title: Introduction to cacheR
text: cacheR provides function-level caching R workflows. Typical use cases basic statistics: Expensive summary statistics large datasets (mean, variance, quantiles). Repeated computation correlation matrices. Multi-step pipelines, e.g. preprocess → summarize → correlate. main idea: Wrap existing function cacheFile(). Call new function usual. cacheR saves results disk reuses inputs function body changed.

title: Introduction to cacheR
text: vignette ’ll use temporary cache directory:
code: library(cacheR) cache_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "cacheR-demo") dir.create(cache_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE) cache_dir #> [1] "/local/vfranke/Tmp/RtmpVJ2w7C/cacheR-demo"

title: Introduction to cacheR
text: Assume function computes several statistics : create cached version: Now, compare first vs second call: First call: prints "Computing stats ..." takes ~1 second. Second call input: almost instant, reusing cached result.
code: stat_summary <- function(x) {   message("Computing stats ...")   Sys.sleep(1)  # pretend it's expensive   x <- na.omit(x)   list(     mean     = mean(x),     variance = var(x),     sd       = sd(x),     median   = median(x)   ) } cached_stat_summary <- cacheFile(   cache_dir = cache_dir,   backend   = "rds" ) %@% function(x) {   stat_summary(x) } set.seed(1) x <- rnorm(1e5)  system.time(res1 <- cached_stat_summary(x)) #> Computing stats ... #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.009   0.000   1.010 system.time(res2 <- cached_stat_summary(x)) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.001   0.000   0.001  res1 #> $mean #> [1] -0.002244083 #>  #> $variance #> [1] 1.007059 #>  #> $sd #> [1] 1.003523 #>  #> $median #> [1] 0.0008011329 identical(res1, res2) #> [1] TRUE

title: Introduction to cacheR
text: Computing full correlation matrix can expensive many variables. Let’s define function : standardizes columns, computes correlation matrix, optionally returns means variances well. Wrap cacheFile(): Test : : First call: actually computes mean, variance correlation. Second call: returns result disk, skipping expensive computation.
code: cor_with_moments <- function(mat) {   message("Computing correlations and moments ...")   Sys.sleep(1)  # simulate expensive work    # Basic checks   stopifnot(is.matrix(mat) || is.data.frame(mat))   mat <- as.matrix(mat)    means     <- colMeans(mat, na.rm = TRUE)   variances <- apply(mat, 2, var, na.rm = TRUE)    corr_mat  <- stats::cor(mat, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")    list(     mean      = means,     variance  = variances,     cor       = corr_mat   ) } cached_cor_with_moments <- cacheFile(   cache_dir = cache_dir,   backend   = "rds" ) %@% function(mat) {   cor_with_moments(mat) } set.seed(123) mat <- matrix(rnorm(200 * 10), ncol = 10)  system.time(res1 <- cached_cor_with_moments(mat)) #> Computing correlations and moments ... #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.004   0.000   1.004 system.time(res2 <- cached_cor_with_moments(mat)) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.000   0.000   0.001  str(res1$cor) #>  num [1:10, 1:10] 1 -0.0277 -0.0302 -0.0464 -0.0513 ... identical(res1$cor, res2$cor) #> [1] TRUE

title: Introduction to cacheR
text: Suppose often apply pre-processing (centering & scaling) computing summary statistics correlations. Define preprocessing function downstream function uses : Now downstream function uses preprocessed data compute means, variances correlations: Run twice: happens: cached_preprocess_matrix() executed cached. analyze_scaled() executed cached. preprocessing step full analysis loaded cache (assuming function bodies input matrix unchanged). version cacheR supports cache tree, can inspect relationships cached calls: see least two nodes: one analyze_scaled(mat2) one cached_preprocess_matrix(mat2)
code: preprocess_matrix <- function(mat) {   message("Preprocessing matrix (center & scale) ...")   Sys.sleep(1)  # simulate expensive step   scale(mat) }  # Cached preprocessing cached_preprocess_matrix <- cacheFile(   cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function(mat) {   preprocess_matrix(mat) } analyze_scaled <- cacheFile(   cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function(mat) {   message("Analyzing scaled data ...")   mat_scaled <- cached_preprocess_matrix(mat)    means     <- colMeans(mat_scaled)   variances <- apply(mat_scaled, 2, var)   corr_mat  <- stats::cor(mat_scaled)    list(     mean     = means,     variance = variances,     cor      = corr_mat   ) } set.seed(321) mat2 <- matrix(rnorm(300 * 8), ncol = 8)  system.time(a1 <- analyze_scaled(mat2)) #> Analyzing scaled data ... #> Preprocessing matrix (center & scale) ... #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.006   0.000   1.007 system.time(a2 <- analyze_scaled(mat2)) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.001   0.000   0.001  identical(a1$cor, a2$cor) #> [1] TRUE cacheTree_reset() a <- analyze_scaled(mat2) nodes <- cacheTree_nodes() nodes #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d` #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$id #> [1] "analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d" #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$fname #> [1] "analyze_scaled" #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$hash #> [1] "88c7ed8bc6ae408d" #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$outfile #> [1] "/local/vfranke/Tmp/RtmpVJ2w7C/cacheR-demo/analyze_scaled.88c7ed8bc6ae408d.qs" #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$parents #> character(0) #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$children #> character(0) #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$files #> character(0) #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$file_hashes #> character(0) #>  #> $`analyze_scaled:88c7ed8bc6ae408d`$created #> [1] "2025-12-02 16:31:40 CET"

what: 5. Using the qs backend for faster serialization
title: Introduction to cacheR
text: large matrices, qs backend can faster base RDS. Install : create cached function backend = "qs": Compare timings: see .qs files cache directory.
code: install.packages("qs") cached_cor_qs <- cacheFile(   cache_dir = cache_dir,   backend   = "qs" ) %@% function(mat) {   cor_with_moments(mat) } set.seed(999) mat_big <- matrix(rnorm(500 * 50), ncol = 50)  system.time(r1 <- cached_cor_qs(mat_big)) #> Computing correlations and moments ... #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.017   0.000   1.019 system.time(r2 <- cached_cor_qs(mat_big)) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.001   0.000   0.001  identical(r1$cor, r2$cor) #> [1] TRUE list.files(cache_dir, recursive = TRUE) #> [1] "analyze_scaled.88c7ed8bc6ae408d.qs"           #> [2] "cached_cor_qs.9a5887591cc30d22.qs"            #> [3] "cached_cor_with_moments.458b9d5fd0c6e8da.rds" #> [4] "cached_preprocess_matrix.eb193d19e30e3ec8.qs" #> [5] "cached_stat_summary.d199ddc8223d7981.rds"

title: Introduction to cacheR
text: Create example CSV file practice, often: Read data file (CSV, RDS, etc.), Compute summary statistics correlations. can cache steps. Usage: First run: reads CSV computes stats. Subsequent runs file code: reuse cached results. (Depending cacheR version, may options file_args incorporate directory file contents cache key explicitly.)
code: # We use tempdir() so that the vignette can be built anywhere # without needing any external data files.  set.seed(1)  # Small numeric toy dataset my_data <- data.frame(   x = rnorm(100, mean = 0, sd = 1),   y = rnorm(100, mean = 5, sd = 2),   z = rnorm(100, mean = -2, sd = 0.5) )  # Path where we'll save the CSV example_csv <- file.path(tempdir(), "my_data.csv")  # Write the CSV (no row names to keep it clean) write.csv(my_data, example_csv, row.names = FALSE) read_numeric_csv <- function(path) {   message("Reading CSV: ", path)   Sys.sleep(1)  # simulate slow disk / network   read.csv(path) }  cached_read_numeric_csv <- cacheFile(   cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function(path) {   read_numeric_csv(path) }  cached_file_stats <- cacheFile(   cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function(path) {   df <- cached_read_numeric_csv(path)   cor_with_moments(df) } res <- cached_file_stats(example_csv) #> Reading CSV: /local/vfranke/Tmp/RtmpVJ2w7C/my_data.csv #> Computing correlations and moments ... res$mean #>          x          y          z  #>  0.1088874  4.9243838 -1.9851632 res$cor #>               x             y           z #> x  1.0000000000 -0.0009943199  0.01838219 #> y -0.0009943199  1.0000000000 -0.04953621 #> z  0.0183821868 -0.0495362135  1.00000000

title: Introduction to cacheR
text: Use project-local cache directory, e.g.: Add .gitignore: Wrap expensive operations: reading large files computing big correlation matrices heavy preprocessing pure statistical pipelines, common pattern : cached_read() – load data cached_preprocess() – clean / transform cached_stats() – compute means, variances, correlations step can shared across many analyses.
code: cache_dir <- ".cacheR" dir.create(cache_dir, showWarnings = FALSE) .cacheR/

title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: document describes use cacheR package cache expensive steps RNA-seq data analysis pipeline. focuses : Importing RNA-seq count files disk Building DESeqDataSet Fitting DESeq model Automatically invalidating caches : Function arguments change Function code changes number files key directories changes Hardcoded global-variable paths change examples assume working inside R package project includes cacheFile() decorator cacheR.

title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: cacheR caching dependency tracking DESeq2 differential expression analysis dplyr, readr data handling

what: 1.2 Working directories We use a temporary working directory for demonstration. In a real project, you would use a project-specific path.
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
code: work_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "cacheR_rnaseq_vignette")  dir.create(work_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)   counts_dir <- file.path(work_dir, "counts")  dir.create(counts_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)   cache_dir <- file.path(work_dir, "cache")  dir.create(cache_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

what: 2. Simulated RNA-seq data To keep the example self-contained, we simulate small count tables as if they were generated by tools such as featureCounts or htseq-count.
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: point: - counts_dir contains 4 tab-separated count files, one per sample. - sample_table describes experimental design (two conditions: B).
code: set.seed(1)   genes <- paste0("Gene", sprintf("%03d", 1:500))   simulate_sample <- function(sample_id, n_genes = length(genes)) {      data.frame(          gene_id = genes,          count = rpois(n_genes, lambda = sample(10:1000, 1)), stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  }           sample_ids <- paste0("sample", 1:4)   for (sid in sample_ids) {      tab <- simulate_sample(sid)      readr::write_tsv(tab, file.path(counts_dir, paste0(sid, "_counts.tsv")) )  }  sample_table <- data.frame( sample_id = sample_ids, condition = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 2), stringsAsFactors = FALSE )

previous_headings: cacheR RNA-seq Caching Example > 3. Reading count files from a directory
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: following function reads *_counts.tsv files directory, merges gene_id, orders columns match sample table.
code: read_counts_from_dir <- function(count_dir, sample_table) {  files <- list.files(count_dir, pattern = "_counts\\.tsv$", full.names = TRUE)  if (length(files) == 0L) {          stop("No count files found in: ", count_dir)  }  sample_ids <- gsub("_counts\\.tsv$", "", basename(files))  counts_list <- lapply(files, function(f) {          readr::read_tsv(f, show_col_types = FALSE)  })  merged <- counts_list[[1]][, "gene_id", drop = FALSE]  for (i in seq_along(counts_list)) {          merged[[sample_ids[i]]] <- counts_list[[i]]$count }          merged <- merged[, c("gene_id", sample_table$sample_id), drop = FALSE]          merged  }

previous_headings: cacheR RNA-seq Caching Example > 3. Reading count files from a directory
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: now wrap read_counts_from_dir() cacheFile(). key point argument file_args = "count_dir": - cacheR include number files count_dir part cache key. - new files added removed, hash changes function re-run.
code: cached_read_counts <- cacheR:::cacheFile( cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function(count_dir, sample_table) {      read_counts_from_dir(count_dir, sample_table)  }

previous_headings: cacheR RNA-seq Caching Example > 3. Reading count files from a directory
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
code: # First run: reads from disk and caches counts1 <- cached_read_counts(counts_dir, sample_table)

what: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: TRUE
code: counts2 <- cached_read_counts(counts_dir, sample_table)   identical(counts1, counts2) #> [1] TRUE

previous_headings: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache
what: 4. Automatic invalidation when new samples appear
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: add new sample counts file counts_dir, cache invalidated number files count_dir changed. dimensions adding sample5 dim(counts3) dimensions adding sample5 Key behavior: - cached_read_counts() detects counts_dir now 5 files instead 4. - cache key changes, function re-run. - counts3 now contains additional sample5 column.
code: new_id <- "sample5"  sample_table2 <- bind_rows( sample_table, data.frame(sample_id = new_id, condition = "A") )  tab_new <- simulate_sample(new_id)  readr::write_tsv(tab_new, file.path(counts_dir, paste0(new_id, "_counts.tsv")) )  counts3 <- cached_read_counts(counts_dir, sample_table2)   dim(counts1) #> [1] 500   5

title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: Constructing DESeqDataSet can computationally expensive large matrices. can cache step well.

previous_headings: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache > 5. Building a DESeqDataSet with caching
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
code: dds_from_counts <- cacheR:::cacheFile( cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function(counts_tbl, sample_table) {                  mat <- as.matrix(counts_tbl[, sample_table$sample_id])                   rownames(mat) <- counts_tbl$gene_id                   coldata <- as.data.frame(sample_table)                   rownames(coldata) <- coldata$sample_id          DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(                  countData = mat,                  colData   = coldata,                  design    = ~condition          )           }

previous_headings: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache > 5. Building a DESeqDataSet with caching
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: Re-running dds_from_counts(counts3, sample_table2) unchanged inputs load cache instead recomputing.
code: dds <- dds_from_counts(counts3, sample_table2) #> converting counts to integer mode #> Warning in DESeqDataSet(se, design = design, ignoreRank): some variables in #> design formula are characters, converting to factors dds #> class: DESeqDataSet  #> dim: 500 5  #> metadata(1): version #> assays(1): counts #> rownames(500): Gene001 Gene002 ... Gene499 Gene500 #> rowData names(0): #> colnames(5): sample1 sample2 sample3 sample4 sample5 #> colData names(2): sample_id condition  # standard DESeq2 object

title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: Fitting DESeq model often slowest step pipeline.

previous_headings: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache > 6. Caching the DESeq model fit
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: run run_deseq(dds) dds, use cached result rather re-fitting model.
code: run_deseq <- cacheR:::cacheFile( cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function(dds) {          DESeq(dds)          }  dds_fit <- run_deseq(dds) #> estimating size factors #> estimating dispersions #> gene-wise dispersion estimates #> mean-dispersion relationship #> -- note: fitType='parametric', but the dispersion trend was not well captured by the #>    function: y = a/x + b, and a local regression fit was automatically substituted. #>    specify fitType='local' or 'mean' to avoid this message next time. #> final dispersion estimates #> fitting model and testing

what: 6.2 Extracting results Extracting results from the fitted model is typically fast, so you may or may not want to cache it:
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR

previous_headings: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache
what: 7. Tracking hardcoded directories and global variables
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: cacheR can also track directories passed function arguments, appear : Hardcoded calls like list.files("/path//data") Global variable calls like dir(GLOBAL_DIR)

previous_headings: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache > 7. Tracking hardcoded directories and global variables
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: : - function body uses GLOBAL_DIR inside list.files() dir(). - cacheR inspects function body, detects directory usages, includes corresponding file counts cache key. - new file added GLOBAL_DIR, cache entry becomes invalid, hardcoded_fun() re-run.
code: GLOBAL_DIR <- counts_dir   hardcoded_fun <- cacheR:::cacheFile( cache_dir = cache_dir ) %@% function() {          # Using a global variable as directory path          n1 <- length(list.files(GLOBAL_DIR))          n2 <- length(dir(GLOBAL_DIR))          c(literal = n1, global = n2)  }  # First call: computes and caches   hardcoded_fun() #> literal  global  #>       5       5  # Add another file to the directory   extra <- simulate_sample("extra")   readr::write_tsv(extra, file.path(GLOBAL_DIR, "extra_counts.tsv") )  # Second call: cache is invalidated and recomputed   hardcoded_fun() #> literal  global  #>       6       6

previous_headings: Second run: same arguments and same files → uses cache
title: Reproducible and Fast RNA-seq Analysis with cacheR
text: store cache files Use project-specific directory, example cache/ project root, configurable location via options(cacheR.dir = ...). Choosing cache Good candidates : Disk /O heavy operations (reading many files) Large object construction (DESeqDataSet, SummarizedExperiment, etc.) Expensive model fitting steps - cache fast operations (simple filtering, small transforms) rarely benefit caching can add overhead.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Vedran Franke. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Franke V (2025). cacheR: Recursive File-Based Caching Call Tree Introspection. R package version 0.1.0.
code: @Manual{,   title = {cacheR: Recursive File-Based Caching With Call Tree Introspection},   author = {Vedran Franke},   year = {2025},   note = {R package version 0.1.0}, }

title: cacheR
text: cacheR provides recursive, file-based function caching R, unique ability record parent–child relationships cached calls. useful : Building provenance graphs Debugging nested function calls Understanding function tree produces result Reusing heavy computations safely transparently package introduces: cacheFile() — caching decorator %@% — operator applying decorators cacheTree_*() — functions inspecting cache tree

title: cacheR
text: Basic usage
code: # install from GitHub # requires: remotes::install_github() remotes::install_github("BIMSBbioinfo/cacheR") library(cacheR)  cache_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "cache_test") dir.create(cache_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)  # Define cached functions inner <- cacheFile(cache_dir) %@% function(x) x + 1 outer <- cacheFile(cache_dir) %@% function(x) inner(x) * 2  # Execute outer(3) #> 8

what: Create a disk-backed caching decorator for a function — cacheFile
title: Create a disk-backed caching decorator for a function — cacheFile
text: cacheFile() creates decorator caches function results files cache_dir, keyed function body arguments. also records parent-child relationships cached calls "cache tree" can reconstructed.

title: Create a disk-backed caching decorator for a function — cacheFile
code: cacheFile(   cache_dir = NULL,   backend = getOption("cacheR.backend", "rds"),   file_args = NULL )

title: Create a disk-backed caching decorator for a function — cacheFile
text: cache_dir Directory cache files stored. ... Additional options controlling cache behavior (e.g. hashing, compression, invalidation rules).

title: Create a disk-backed caching decorator for a function — cacheFile
text: decorator object can combined function using %@%, returning cached version function.

title: Create a disk-backed caching decorator for a function — cacheFile
text: Typical usage (%@% decorator operator) :
code: inner_fun <- cacheFile(cache_dir) %@% function(x) x + 1 outer_fun <- cacheFile(cache_dir) %@% function(x) inner_fun(x) * 2

title: Return cache tree nodes — cacheTree_nodes
text: Returns list/data.frame describing nodes currently recorded cache tree (cached call instance).

title: Return cache tree nodes — cacheTree_nodes
code: cacheTree_nodes()

title: Return cache tree nodes — cacheTree_nodes
text: object representing nodes (e.g. data.frame id, fn_label, key, cache_file, etc.).

what: Reset the cache tree state — cacheTree_reset
title: Reset the cache tree state — cacheTree_reset
text: Clears recorded parent-child relationships cached calls. (default) delete files disk (handled separately, e.g. cleaning cache_dir).

title: Reset the cache tree state — cacheTree_reset
code: cacheTree_reset()

what: Turn a function into a decorator. — decorator
title: Turn a function into a decorator. — decorator
text: Turn function decorator.

title: Turn a function into a decorator. — decorator
code: decorator(f)

what: Combine two decorators lazily into a new decorator. — .delayed_decorate
title: Combine two decorators lazily into a new decorator. — .delayed_decorate
text: Combine two decorators lazily new decorator.

title: Combine two decorators lazily into a new decorator. — .delayed_decorate
code: .delayed_decorate(d1, d2, decorator_calls)
